content.problemStatement:
**Q5) B.:** A project management tool needs to automatically tag tasks with the current week number of the year for scheduling purposes. Your task is to create a Python program that determines and displays the week number of the year for today's date.

You must use the `datetime` module to accomplish this. The week number should be calculated based on the ISO 8601 standard (weeks start on Monday, and week 1 is the first week with a Thursday).


Output Format
The program should print a single integer representing the current week number of the year.
content.inputFormat: Optional first line contains an integer T (number of test cases). If the line is absent, T defaults to 1. No other input is read.
content.outputFormat: Print the ISO‑8601 week number of the current date as a decimal integer. If T>1, print the same number on T separate lines.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The program is executed on a date that belongs to ISO week 42, so it prints 42 once (default T=1).
The first line indicates T=3, therefore the current week number (42) is printed three times, each on its own line.

solution.approach: The ISO‑8601 week number can be obtained directly from the standard library of each language. Steps: 1) Read the whole input, try to parse the first token as an integer T; if parsing fails, set T = 1. 2) Query the current local date. 3) Convert the date to its ISO week number (C/C++: strftime with "%V", Python: date.isocalendar()[1], Java: WeekFields.ISO.weekOfWeekBasedYear(), JavaScript: custom function based on the algorithm described in ISO‑8601). 4) Output the week number T times, each on its own line. The algorithm is O(1) per test case and uses O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are stored.
